pages.text: Laboratory evaluation of antioxidants for asphalt binders Alex K. Apeagyei * Virginia Transportation Research Council, Charlottesville, Virginia, USA article info Article history: Received 27 January 2010 Received in revised form 20 April 2010 Accepted 19 June 2010 Available online 13 July 2010 Keywords: Asphalt Oxidative aging Age-hardening Antioxidants Aging index abstract A laboratory study was carried to investigate whether certain antioxidants could be used to reduce age- hardening in asphalt binders. Several additives including: Vitamin E, Irganox 1010, Irgafos P-EPQ, carbon black, hydrated lime, and DLTDP/furfural, were evaluated. The modiﬁed asphalt binders were subjected to two accelerated oxidative aging regimes in the laboratory. Short-term oxidative aging of the binders was simulated using a rolling thin ﬁlm oven while long-term aging was performed using a pressure aging vessel. The effects of aging on the binders were evaluated based on changes in rheological properties of asphalt binders undergoing oxidative aging. Binders modiﬁed with a combination of furfural and DLTDP was found to posses the lowest aging index. The antioxidant-treated binders exhibited improved perfor- mance at both high and low pavement temperatures. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Asphalt binders get stiffer and more brittle with age. This phe- nomenon is commonly called aging or age-hardening. Several fac- tors have been cited to explain the phenomenon of age-hardening in asphalt. The major causes of age-hardening commonly cited in- clude oxidation, volatilization, polymerization, thixotropy, synere- sis, and separation [1–4]. Among these factors, oxidation and volatilization are generally considered as the most important fac- tors affecting aging in asphalt paving mixtures. Oxidation is the irreversible chemical reaction of atmospheric oxygen with asphalt. Oxidation of asphalt can occur throughout the life of an asphalt pavement such as during mixing, ﬁeld place- ment, and during service. The rate and amount of oxidation de- pends on factors such as chemical composition of the asphalt, environmental conditioning, and length of exposure to weathering. Age-hardening of paving grade asphalts is a major factor affect- ing durability bituminous pavement. Many highway and airﬁeld pavements fail in service prematurely due in part to excessive hardening of the asphalt binder as a result of oxidation. Excessive hardening and brittleness caused by oxidation during hot-mix as- phalt (HMA) production and also during service increase the sus- ceptibility of asphalt pavements to cracking. Cracked pavements allow water to percolate into the pavement structure to weaken the pavement base and hasten pavement deterioration. Reduction of oxidative hardening using antioxidants could lead to longer last- ing pavement and substantial savings in life cycle cost. Several antioxidants have been used in the past to control oxi- dative hardening in asphalt pavements [5–25]. Initial laboratory investigations of these antioxidants showed some promise. How- ever, most of these antioxidant systems for retarding oxidative hardening have not performed satisfactorily in the ﬁeld due to problems such as degradation, volatility, and loss of the antioxi- dant from the asphalt system. Another problem associated with some of the currently used antioxidants is that their use results in tender mixes since the antioxidants often soften the binder excessively. Th

study was limited to analysis of physical measurements for sim- plicity and also to be in conformity with previous studies on as- phalt aging. Aging index computations based on the single-point measure- ments are one of the most common procedures used to estimate aging and provide ranking among different asphalt binders. Aging index is deﬁned as the ratio of a given binder property after a given level of aging to that before aging as shown in Eq. (1). The most common parameter for computing aging index of asphalt binders has historically been based on viscosity measured at 60 /C176C. Kandhal et al. [27] reported that aging indices computed using apparent vis- cosity at 25 /C176C and 0.05 s /C0 1 shear rate correlated best with age- hardening and asphalt pavement durability in Pennsylvania. It was reported that ravelling appeared only on pavements with aging index values of 12 or higher after long-term aging. As previously noted, aging index can be computed based on any asphalt physical property that changes with aging. Shown in Fig. 1 is a comparison of aging index using viscosity and SUPERPAVE rut- ting parameter G/C3 /sin d for 37 different asphalt binders form the Materials Reference Lab [28]. The results suggest relationship be- tween viscosity and G/C3 /sin d is quite good. Screening of antioxidant evaluated in this study was therefore based on G/C3 /sin d aging index instead of viscosity-based aging index. Aging index ¼ Physical property of aged asphalt Physical property of original asphalt ð1Þ 2. Purpose and scope The primary objective of this study was to determine if asphalt age-hardening could be controlled using antioxidants. Effective antioxidants for asphalt stabilization cannot be predicted a priori; all new potential antioxidants will have to be tested to determine their effectiveness in controlling asphalt aging. The effectiveness of antioxidants are not linearly related to concentration, therefore several antioxidants at various concentrations were tested and screened during the study. The scope of work included modifying paving grade asphalt binders with selected anti-aging additives, subjecting the modiﬁed binders to accelerated oxidative aging in the laboratory, and eval- uating the effect of aging on the modiﬁed binders through rheolog- ical measurements. Aging indices based on the measured rheological parameters were used as screening tools for ranking the effectiveness of the additives used. A single PG 64-22 perfor- mance graded asphalt binder was during the screening stage to se- lect the most promising antioxidant. The effectiveness of the most promising antioxidant treatment was veriﬁed using additional but different asphalt binders. Computed aging indices were compared with 42 other binders from published data from the Materials Ref- erence Laboratory [28]. 3. Materials and methods 3.1. Identiﬁcation of suitable antioxidants Several additives were reviewed for inclusion in the study. The major criteria used in selecting these antioxidants were that the selected antioxidants posses both lower volatility as well as higher resistance to extraction during hot-mix production and under service conditions. Some of the selected antioxidants have never been used for controlling oxidative aging of asphalt mixes. 3.1.1. Selection of antioxidants Two primary antioxidants Irganox 1010 and Irganox E201 (Vitamin E) produced by Ciba Specialty Chemicals were evaluated. Irganox 1010 was selected because it has been reported to

A Barnant blender with a 2-in. blade operating at a speed of 750 rpm was used to incorporate the antioxidants into the base asphalt. Mixing percentages varied from 0.2% to 10% w/w of the base asphalt. Combinations of various antioxidants were also evaluated to determine whether synergistic behaviour existed between any of the antioxidants. Mixing temperatures for all antioxidants were kept at less than 125 /C176C to avoid excessive aging of the binders. Mixing times ranged from about a minute to about four hours depending on the type of antioxidant. Most of the addi- tives were readily miscible with asphalt at 125 /C176C. Modiﬁcation of the binders using the DLTDP/furfural combination required the longest mixing time of four hours. 3.3. Simulation of oxidative aging Both short-term and long-term oxidative aging of asphalt binders were simu- lated in the laboratory. Short-term aging was conducted using the rolling thin ﬁlm oven (RTFO) according to standard AASHTO T240 procedures. A James Cox & Sons model CS 325A RTFO was used. During the RTFO test, a 35 g asphalt sample in the form of 1.25 mm thin ﬁlm of asphalt was exposed to both heat and air ﬂowing at the rate 400 ml/min for 85 min in an oven (163 /C176C) with a rotating rack revolving at speed of 15 rpm. The RTFO test is a short-term aging procedure for asphalt bind- ers which simulate the aging that takes during hot-mix production and about 2 years of in-ﬁeld aging. Long-term aging was also simulated in the laboratory using a pressure aging vessel (PAV). The PAV is a standard test that simulates the aging of asphalt binder after about 10 years in the ﬁeld. During the PAV testing, a 50 g sample of RTFO-aged sample is subjected to pressurized air (2.1 MPa) at a temperature of 100 /C176C for 20 h. Residue from the PAV test is tested using a dynamic shear rheometer (DSR) to eval- uate fatigue performance and a bending beam rheometer (BBR) to evaluate low temperature cracking. 3.4. Rheological testing Rheological testing was conducted using a Bohlin dynamic shear rheometer and an ATS bending beam rheometer . The DSR was operated under constant stress mode at a frequency of 10 rad/s using AASHTO T315 procedures. DSR tests were con- ducted on both short-term and long-term aged asphalt binders. Tests on the un- aged and RTFO-aged samples were tested at a temperature of 64 /C176C using a 1 mm thick asphalt sample and a 25 mm diameter spindle to determine the SUPERPAVE rutting parameter G /C3 /sin d. DSR rheological testing were also conducted at interme- diate pavement temperature of 25 /C176C to fatigue parameter G/C3 sin d and at /C0 12 /C176Ct o evaluate low temperature cracking. An ATS BBR was used to determine the rheological response of the binders at /C0 12 /C176C. PAV-aged materials were used to fabricate a 101.6 /C2 12.7 /C2 6.25 mm beam required for BBR testing. The beams were kept in a temperature chamber at /C0 12 /C176C (the recommended testing temperature for PG 64-22 binders) for one hour before testing. During BBR testing, a constant creep load of approximately 960 mN was ap- plied for a total period of 240 s. For each modiﬁed binder, two replicate beams were tested. Two parameters of interest measured during the BBR testing are the creep stiffness and the m-value. The creep stiffness and the slope of log creep stiffness versus log time curve ( m-value) at 60 s are the required speciﬁcation values (AASH- TO T313-02). Testing conducted on short-term aged binder at 64 /C176C wa

modify binders in the presence of a catalyst. The treatment re- sulted in an aging index of about 1.44 or less which represent over 40% reduction in age-hardening of the modiﬁed binders compared with the unmodiﬁed binders. This aging level was signiﬁcantly lower than all the additives considered. 4.2. Discussion of preliminary screening tests results At the testing temperature of 64 /C176C and testing frequency (10 rad/s) used during DSR testing, all the asphalt binders exhib- ited higher G/C3 /sin d values with aging. Compared to the control or unmodiﬁed binders, the carbon black-modiﬁed binders showed progressively greater stiffness ( G /C3 /sin d) as the concentration of carbon black was increased from 0.5% to 5%. The effect of Irganox 1010 modiﬁcation on G/C3 /sin d was not signiﬁcant for Irganox 1010 concentrations of 1% or less, however greater concentration (5% and above) resulted in a decrease in stiffness compared to the control or carbon black. Substantially lower stiffness values were observed for the samples modiﬁed with Vitamin E (1–6%) compared to the control, carbon black, Irganox 1010, Irgafos or hy- drated lime. Mixtures of the various antioxidants in combinations were also evaluated to determine if synergistic behaviour existed between any of the antioxidants. No synergistically beneﬁcial effects were observed in mixtures containing Vitamin E and hydrated lime. Compared to Vitamin E acting alone, mixtures of Vitamin E and carbon black showed no improvement in aging stability. The preli- minary tests as described above showed that all the antioxidants evaluated were readily miscible with the base asphalt at the tem- peratures and concentrations used. Based on the results of the preliminary screening test results, a list of the more promising antioxidant systems (i.e. those with low- er aging index than the control asphalt binder) was made. Addi- tional testing was conducted to evaluate the impact of the promising antioxidants identiﬁed in the initial screening testing on long-term PAV aging. The idea was that asphalt binder perfor- mance after short-term aging could be used as a harbinger of long-term aging behaviour. 4.3. Final binder screening tests As indicated previously, modiﬁed samples with AI less than 2.00 after short-term RTFO-aging were selected based on the fact that the average AI for the control binder was 2.32. Samples of selected modiﬁed having AI less than 2.00 were prepared and subjected to both short-term (RTFO) and long-term aging in a pressure aging vessel (PAV). The long-term aged samples were tested at two temperatures (intermediate and cold temperatures) using DSR and bending beam rheometer to evaluate effect on aging. At the intermediate pavement temperature of 25 /C176C, the DSR was used to measure the fatigue parameter G /C3 sin d using an 8 mm spindle and a 2 mm thick binder sample. At least three replicate 2 mm thick DSR sam- ples each were tested for every modiﬁed binder tested. Lower fatigue parameter values have been correlated with high- er fatigue life of asphalt pavements. A limiting value of 5 MPa is speciﬁed under standard SUPERPAVE procedures. Table 2 details the results of DSR testing performed at 25 /C176C on long-term aged binders. The results represent average fatigue parameter for three replicate specimens of each binder. Also shown in Table 2 are the COV values obtained. Analyses of the results indicate sample S13 was signiﬁcantly softer than the control binder. It should be no

In general most of the antioxidants had lower aging index val- ues compared with the control binder. The antioxidant treatment involving the DLTDP/furfural combination had the lowest aging in- dex values at after short-term and long-term aging as indicated by the lower AI obtained at both the intermediate and high testing temperatures. The combinations of DLTDP/furfural when used to treat the control PG 64-22 asphalt binder were found to be most effective in reducing oxidative aging of all the modiﬁers considered in this paper. This DLTDP/furfural combination also possessed the desirable properties both low temperatures (lower stiffness) and high temperatures (high stiffness). 4.4. Veriﬁcation of most promising additives in other binders Since results for only one type of PG 64-22 asphalt binders from Illinois was used in the initial screening phase of the study as dis- cussed in the foregoing sections, it was felt that additional testing with binders from different sources should be evaluated to verify the effectiveness of the DLTDP/furfural treatment. Additional bind- ers not used in the initial screening tests were therefore selected for testing to determine if the most promising additives were effec- tive in other asphalt binders. They included a PG 64-22 from a source in Wisconsin; and a reference binder AAD-1 from the Mate- rials Reference Laboratory, MRL (28). The objective of this round of testing was to verify the efﬁcacy of the DLTDP/furfural combina- tion to reduce aging in binders from different sources and different grades. This is important since previous studies suggest certain antioxidants may be effective in one binder and completely inef- fective in others. The modiﬁed binders were subjected to the usual short-term and long-term aging in the laboratory as previously discussed. DSR was used to measure stiffness at both high and intermediate pavement temperatures while a bending beam rheometer was used to evaluate the low temperature stiffness of the modiﬁed mixtures. Speciﬁcally, the DSR was used to estimate the rutting parameter at three temperatures (64 /C176C, 70 /C176C, and 76 /C176C); and the fatigue parameter at two temperatures (22 /C176C and 25 /C176C); while the BBR was used to /C0 12 /C176C and /C0 18 /C176C to measure creep stiffness. Testing at these multiple temperatures afforded the exact PG grade of the modiﬁed binders to be estimated and the beneﬁt the modi- ﬁcation to be quantiﬁed. The results are summarized in Table 4 . From Table 4 it could be seen that aging index obtained after short-term aging of the modiﬁed binders were signiﬁcantly lower than the unmodiﬁed binders. Also the range of aging index values compares with those obtained with the binder from Illinois used during the initial screening tests. Similar results were observed for long-term aged binders where aging index (based on fatigue parameter) ranged from 2.88 to 3.36 and 5.13 and 5.55 for the modiﬁed and unmodiﬁed binders respectively. Thus the DLTDP/ furfural modiﬁcation resulted in over 40% reduction in age-harden- ing of treated binders which compare with those obtained for the Illinois binder used earlier. The results suggest the DLTDP/furfural antioxidant treatment identiﬁed in this study may be effective in binders from different sources. Another observation was that mod- iﬁcation using the approach presented in this paper results in sig- niﬁcantly stiffer binders at high pavement temperatures and signiﬁcantly lower stiffness at lo

5. Summary and conclusions The effects of the antioxidants on age-hardening were evalu- ated by measuring the rheological properties of asphalt binders before and after selected levels of aging. Short-term and long- term aging were simulated using the rolling thin ﬁlm oven at 163 /C176C and the pressure aging vessel at 100 /C176C respectively for the asphalt binders. The rheological response of the modiﬁed as- phalt binders was evaluated using the dynamic shear rheometer and the bending beam rheometer. The effectiveness of the antiox- idants in reducing age-hardening was evaluated using the AI parameter. Promising antioxidant treatment systems were con- sidered as those with lower aging indices than the control asphalt binder. Based on the results of the binder testing, a combination of DLTDP and furfural acting together in the presence of a catalyst appears to be effective in reducing asphalt age-hardening in as- phalt binders considered in this study. The DLTDP/furfural combi- nation appears to possess both the lowest AI as well as being softer at low temperatures and stiffer at higher temperatures. Selection of the antioxidant combination in this study was based on the performance of the modiﬁed binder across the whole tem- perature spectrum encountered in typical paving applications. The following conclusions could be made based on the results of this study: /C15 The antioxidant treatment involving DLTDP and furfural resulted in about 40% reduction in short-term age-hardening of the binders compared with the control. /C15 Similar to the short-term aging, age-hardening after long-term aging was comparatively lower in the modiﬁed binders than the control: fatigue parameter AI = 3.17 for modiﬁed versus AI = 5.24 for unmodiﬁed. /C15 Unlike antioxidants used in the past, the DLTDP/furfural antiox- idant presented in this study did not result in excessively soft binders that could lead to tender mixes. /C15 Stiffness modulus at 25 /C176C and ﬂexural stiffness modulus at /C0 12 /C176C for the modiﬁed binders were signiﬁcantly lower than the unmodiﬁed binders tested. Thus the mod- iﬁed binders could be expected to have better cracking resistance. Additional studies are recommended to improve and validate the results presented in this study using additional binders as well as asphalt mixtures.
